Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have these bursts where I'd listen to lectures for hours, then go days without touching anything. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often just have lecture recordings playing while doing other stuff around my apartment. There wasn't really a system—I'd just study when I felt like I could focus, which wasn't that often given how anxious I've been feeling.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly. Sometimes I'd cram everything near deadlines, other times I'd randomly get motivated at 2 AM and watch a bunch of videos. I never had a set schedule like 'Tuesdays at 3 PM I study.' It really depended on my headspace that day and whether my anxiety was manageable enough to actually concentrate.
Q3: Usually a deadline reminder or an email notification would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted, scroll around for a bit, then leave without doing much. The guilt of knowing I was falling behind on assignments would occasionally push me to open the LMS, even if I didn't end up being productive.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me. I'd go through phases where I logged in more regularly, but then life would get overwhelming and I'd drop off again. The CBT sessions I've been doing helped me recognize some of these patterns, but I can't say the LMS ever felt like a natural part of my daily routine.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often play them at 1.5x speed and just absorb the information auditorily. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful too because hearing different perspectives sometimes clicked things into place for me that reading alone wouldn't.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and written resources. PDFs and textbook chapters just don't work for my brain—I'd download them with good intentions but never actually read them. The practice quizzes I also avoided because they stressed me out; I'd rather just listen to lectures again than test myself and face potential failure.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was engaging auditorily or if it directly connected to exam content. If I could listen to something and feel like I was actually learning, that was valuable. Busy work or things that felt disconnected from what I'd actually be tested on? Those felt like a waste, especially when I was already struggling to find motivation.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities basically didn't exist in my mind. If something wasn't required, I told myself I'd get to it 'eventually,' which meant never. Required activities at least had that external pressure pushing me. Without a deadline or grade attached, my brain just couldn't prioritize it over everything else competing for my attention.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone while commuting, exercising, or just lying in bed. I also found some YouTube videos and podcasts that covered similar material. A lot of my actual learning happened through audio content that never showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be re-listening to downloaded lectures or talking through concepts out loud to myself—that's how I process things. I'd also sometimes call a classmate and we'd discuss material over the phone for an hour. None of that would show up anywhere, but it was genuinely when I was learning the most.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the audio or find it elsewhere, and then listen to it multiple times offline. My exam scores were pretty good, and a lot of that came from repetitive listening that happened completely outside the platform. One view on the LMS might represent five or six listens in reality.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked, I had some personal stuff going on, and I just couldn't bring myself to engage. The workload felt insurmountable even though I had the resources to handle it. Starting the CBT sessions helped eventually, but there were a few weeks where I basically disappeared from the course entirely.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Even logging in felt like a chore. Competing responsibilities—work stuff, family things—would drain whatever mental energy I had. The platform became associated with stress, so I avoided it even when I knew I shouldn't.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, just spiraling about deadlines or life stuff. I'd technically 'watch' something for thirty minutes and retain absolutely nothing. The anxiety made it hard to actually be present even when I was going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were killing my grade. The anxiety around starting them was paralyzing—I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same way. It's frustrating because I understood the material well enough to ace exams, but assignments felt like this massive mountain I couldn't climb.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then frantically log in the night before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could cut through my avoidance. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. Good feedback on exams motivated me a bit, but critical feedback on assignments sometimes made my anxiety worse and pushed me further away. After the CBT sessions, I got better at not catastrophizing negative feedback, but earlier in the semester it could really derail me for days.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but was actually bad for someone like me. Too much freedom meant I could drift. I needed more scaffolding, more check-ins, something to keep me accountable. The open structure let me fall through the cracks even though I had the capability to succeed.
Q19: Having so many online courses at once—nineteen—made everything blur together. This course didn't stand out enough to grab my attention when I was juggling so much. The video content was good for my learning style, but the sheer volume of materials across all my courses made it easy to neglect things here.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, honestly. High activity might mean they're anxious and checking things repeatedly, or watching videos without actually processing them. They might be struggling to translate engagement into completed work because of mental health stuff, procrastination, or feeling overwhelmed by the gap between understanding and doing.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam scores were great because I genuinely learned the material, mostly through methods that didn't show up in the LMS. I listened to things offline, I thought about concepts while doing other activities, I had conversations with peers. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low learning—it might just mean different learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people log in and what they click on, but they completely miss the quality of engagement or learning that happens elsewhere. They can't measure whether someone was actually focused or just had a tab open. They definitely can't capture offline studying, conversations, or internal processing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through motions. For me, it's when I'm genuinely curious, when concepts stick, when I find myself thinking about course content outside of study time. It's a mental state, not a set of behaviors that can be tracked.
Q24: So much. My anxiety levels, the fact that I was doing CBT to cope, all the offline listening I did, the conversations with classmates, the genuine understanding I developed despite my low assignment completion. The data would show a disengaged student, but I actually learned a lot—I just couldn't always translate that into visible output.
